sections.title: Part 1: Multiple-Choice Cloze
sections.instructions: For questions 1-18, read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.
sections.passageHtml:
é

Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet. ' Se The Cricket Tour As the plane took off for the England cricket tour to Australia, I was facing a challenge. 45 Less than six weeks earlier I had been a professional cricketer, but since then I had and switched camps and joined the press corps. While many other cricketers have retired to and write about the game, none had started their new careers at the sharp end in the world of tabloid journalism. I was only too aware of the manner in which the majority of players viewed the press. 'Vultures,' was what I had heard one call them a few years before, and I wondered how long it would be before I, too, was dismissed in that derogatory fashion. To make matters worse, I knew that my presence had provoked a feeling of resentment in more than one of the journalists on that plane. It was not a personal dislike but concern, based on self-preservation, that a cricketer with little or no training could just saunter in and take an experienced reporter's job. So I was most relieved to find that I was sitting and next to Colin Bateman of the Daily Express, whom I had known for some time. We had become friends. Now, however, we would be in direct competition. I asked him if he was planning to write anything that night. 'Of course," he retorted. "But all we've done is got on a plane." With a wry smile he replied: "Welcome to journalism.'

You are going to read an extract from a novel. Seven paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A-H the one which fits each gap (27-33). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use. e

sections.passageHtml:
Life After Cricket

Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet. ' Se The Cricket Tour As the plane took off for the England cricket tour to Australia, I was facing a challenge. 45 Less than six weeks earlier I had been a professional cricketer, but since then I had and switched camps and joined the press corps. While many other cricketers have retired to and write about the game, none had started their new careers at the sharp end in the world of tabloid journalism. I was only too aware of the manner in which the majority of players viewed the press. 'Vultures,' was what I had heard one call them a few years before, and I wondered how long it would be before I, too, was dismissed in that derogatory fashion. To make matters worse, I knew that my presence had provoked a feeling of resentment in more than one of the journalists on that plane. It was not a personal dislike but concern, based on self-preservation, that a cricketer with little or no training could just saunter in and take an experienced reporter's job. So I was most relieved to find that I was sitting and next to Colin Bateman of the Daily Express, whom I had known for some time. We had become friends. Now, however, we would be in direct competition. I asked him if he was planning to write anything that night. 'Of course," he retorted. "But all we've done is got on a plane." With a wry smile he replied: "Welcome to journalism.'

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: Question 19: What made the writer different from other retired cricketers who had become writers?
options: A. how recent his experience of being a player was
B. the kind of newspaper he had gone to work for
C. his acceptance of the fact that players would now dislike him
D. the nature of his relationships with other cricketers
questions.explanation: Đáp án chính thức: B - the kind of newspaper he had gone to work for. Giải thích biên soạn/đề xuất: đối chiếu option với evidence trong đoạn đọc. Đáp án đúng phải trả lời đúng trọng tâm câu hỏi, khớp phạm vi ý, thái độ người viết và quan hệ logic trong câu/đoạn. Nếu option chỉ lặp keyword nhưng đổi chủ thể, đổi mức độ, đảo nguyên nhân-kết quả hoặc thêm thông tin không có trong bài thì phải loại. Phân tích phương án sai: A (how recent his experience of being a player was): distractor cần loại; hãy đối chiếu lại evidence để tìm lỗi thường gặp như sai phạm vi, không được nêu trực tiếp, đảo ý, hoặc chỉ trùng keyword.; C (his acceptance of the fact that players would now dislike him): distractor cần loại; hãy đối chiếu lại evidence để tìm lỗi thường gặp như sai phạm vi, không được nêu trực tiếp, đảo ý, hoặc chỉ trùng keyword.; D (the nature of his relationships with other cricketers): distractor cần loại; hãy đối chiếu lại evidence để tìm lỗi thường gặp như sai phạm vi, không được nêu trực tiếp, đảo ý, hoặc chỉ trùng keyword.
questions.answerLocation: B. the kind of newspaper he had gone to work for

questions.instruction: Question 20: What does the writer imply about some of the other journalists on the plane?
options: A. They realised that he would have an advantage over them.
B. They instinctively regarded newcomers to their group with suspicion.
C. Their attitude resulted from lack of confidence in their own abilities.

options: A. Some artists are less keen to make use of it than others.

questionGroups.instruction: Match the correct paragraph (A-H) to each gap.

paragraphOptions.label: A
paragraphOptions.text: An entire world could be created in five pages, and one that was more pleasing than a model farm. The childhood of a spoiled prince could be framed within half a page, a moonlit dash through sleepy villages was one rhythmically emphatic sentence, falling in love could be achieved in a single word - a glance. The pages of a recently finished story seemed to vibrate in her hand with all the life they contained.

paragraphOptions.label: B
paragraphOptions.text: A room near Briony's had been dusted down, new curtains had been hung and furniture carried in from other rooms. Normally, she would have been involved in these preparations, but they coincided with her two-day writing bout.

paragraphOptions.label: C
paragraphOptions.text: Only when a story was finished, all fates resolved and the whole matter sealed off at both ends so it resembled, at least in this one respect, every other finished story in the world, could she feel immune, and ready to bind the chapters with string, paint or draw the cover, and take the finished work to show to her mother or her father.

paragraphOptions.label: D
paragraphOptions.text: In any case, she was discovering, as had many writers before her, that not all recognition is helpful. Cecilia's enthusiasm, for example, seemed a little overstated, tainted with condescension perhaps, and intrusive too; her big sister wanted each bound story catalogued and placed on the library shelves, between Rabindranath Tagore and Quintus Tertullian.

paragraphOptions.label: E
paragraphOptions.text: What was unpleasant and distasteful, on the other hand, had infinite variation. A universe reduced to what was said in it was tidiness indeed, almost to the point of nullity, and to compensate, every utterance was delivered at the extremity of some feeling or other, in the service of which the exclamation mark was indispensable.

paragraphOptions.label: F
paragraphOptions.text: But this early attempt showed her that the imagination itself was a source of secrets: while she was writing a story, no one could be told. Pretending in words was too tentative, too vulnerable, too embarrassing to let anyone know.

paragraphOptions.label: G
paragraphOptions.text: Her straight-backed dolls in their many-roomed mansion appeared to be under strict instructions not to touch the walls; the various thumb-sized figures to be found standing about her dressing table suggested by their even ranks and spacing a citizen's army awaiting orders. This taste for the miniature was just one aspect of an orderly spirit.

paragraphOptions.label: H
paragraphOptions.text: Her wish for a harmonious, organised world denied her the reckless possibilities of wrongdoing. Mayhem and destruction were too chaotic for her tastes, and she did not have it in her to be cruel. Her effective status as an only child, as well as the relative isolation of the Tallis house, kept her, at least during the long summer holidays, from girlish intrigues with friends.
